meta.manuscript_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem

preamble.summary: Isaiah 65 contains 25 verses spanning columns L-LI of 1QIsaiah-a. God responds to the lament of chapters 63-64 with a complex answer: He was ready to be found but Israel sought idols instead (vv. 1-7); a remnant will be preserved while rebels perish (vv. 8-16); and the chapter climaxes with the vision of new heavens and a new earth (vv. 17-25). This last section is one of the great eschatological passages of the Hebrew Bible, echoed in Revelation 21:1-4 and 2 Peter 3:13. The scroll shows mostly minor variants with a few moderate readings.
preamble.notable_variants: Verse 1 is quoted in Romans 10:20 and the scroll confirms the MT reading. Verse 17 — 'new heavens and a new earth' — is stable between both texts. Verse 20 has a moderate variant in the description of lifespans in the new creation. Verse 25 echoes Isaiah 11:6-9 with the wolf and lamb imagery, preserved without variant.
preamble.scroll_condition: Columns L-LI are in good condition. The text is fully legible throughout this chapter.

verses.mt_reading: נִדְרַשְׁתִּי לְלוֹא שָׁאָלוּ נִמְצֵאתִי לְלֹא בִקְשֻׁנִי
verses.dss_reading: נדרשתי ללוא שאלו נמצאתי ללוא בקשוני
verses.variant_rendering: I was ready to be sought by those who did not ask; I was ready to be found by those who did not seek Me
verses.mt_rendering: I was ready to be sought by those who did not ask; I was ready to be found by those who did not seek Me
variant_notes: The scroll spells lo with waw-aleph (לוא) in both occurrences, per Qumran convention. The substantive reading is identical. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:20, applying it to Gentile inclusion: 'I was found by those who did not seek Me; I revealed Myself to those who did not ask for Me.' The scroll confirms that the Hebrew text Paul was drawing on (via the LXX) had the same reading as MT.

In its original Third Isaiah context, this verse likely refers to Israel's own failure to seek God properly, but Paul's re-reading applies it to the Gentile mission. The scroll provides no variant that would support or undermine either reading.

verses.variant_rendering: I spread out My hands all the day
verses.mt_rendering: I spread out My hands all the day
variant_notes: The scroll spells kol ('all') with waw as kol (כול). Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:21. God's gesture of outstretched hands toward a rebellious people is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: a people who provoke Me
verses.mt_rendering: a people who provoke Me
variant_notes: The scroll uses a fuller spelling for the participle hamakh'isim ('who provoke'). The catalogue of idolatrous practices — sacrificing in gardens and burning incense on bricks — is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: who sit in tombs
verses.mt_rendering: who sit in tombs
variant_notes: The scroll uses plene spelling for yoshvim ('those sitting'). The description of necromantic practices — sitting in tombs, spending nights in secret places, eating swine's flesh — is identical in both texts. These practices would have been particularly offensive to the Qumran community.

verses.dss_reading: קרב אליכה אל תגש בי כיא קדשתיכה
verses.variant_rendering: Keep to yourself, do not come near me, for I am too holy for you
verses.mt_rendering: Keep to yourself, do not come near me, for I am too holy for you
variant_notes: The scroll uses fuller suffix forms -kha and spells ki as kia. The ironic self-righteousness of the idolaters who say 'I am too holy for you' provokes God's declaration: 'These are a smoke in My nostrils, a fire that burns all the day.' Both texts agree.
No significant variant. God's declaration that judgment is written before Him and He will repay is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: your iniquities and your fathers' iniquities
verses.mt_rendering: your iniquities and your fathers' iniquities
variant_notes: The scroll uses plene spelling for awonot ('iniquities'). The punishment encompassing both their own and their fathers' sins is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: as when new wine is found in the cluster
verses.mt_rendering: as when new wine is found in the cluster
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. The grape cluster metaphor — do not destroy it, for there is a blessing in it — introduces the remnant theology. God will not destroy the whole for the sake of the faithful.
No significant variant. The promise of offspring from Jacob and an inheritor of God's mountains is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: Sharon shall be a pasture for flocks
verses.mt_rendering: Sharon shall be a pasture for flocks
variant_notes: The scroll uses plene spelling for tso'n ('flocks'). The pastoral vision of Sharon and the Valley of Achor as places of rest for flocks is identical in both texts.

verses.mt_reading: הַשֹּׁכְחִים אֶת־הַר קָדְשִׁי הַעֹרְכִים לַגַּד שֻׁלְחָן
verses.dss_reading: השוכחים את הר קודשי העורכים לגד שלחן
verses.variant_rendering: who forget My holy mountain, who set a table for Gad
verses.mt_rendering: who forget My holy mountain, who set a table for Gad
variant_notes: The scroll uses plene spellings for shokchim ('those forgetting') and qodshi ('My holiness'). The reference to 'Gad' (Fortune) and 'Meni' (Destiny) — pagan deities worshipped through ritual meals — is identical in both texts. These deities of fate/fortune are otherwise barely attested in the Hebrew Bible.

verses.variant_rendering: I will destine you to the sword
verses.mt_rendering: I will destine you to the sword
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. The wordplay between Meni (the pagan deity of Destiny) and manithi ('I will destine') is preserved in both texts — God puns on the idol's name to declare judgment.
No significant variant. The contrast between God's servants (who eat, drink, and rejoice) and the rebels (who hunger, thirst, and are put to shame) is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: from gladness of heart
verses.mt_rendering: from gladness of heart
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. The contrast continues: God's servants sing from gladness of heart, but the rebels cry out from pain of heart and wail from brokenness of spirit.

verses.variant_rendering: you shall leave your name as a curse to My chosen ones
verses.mt_rendering: you shall leave your name as a curse to My chosen ones
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. The rebels' name becomes a curse formula, while God's servants receive a new name. The concept of divine renaming connects to 62:2.

verses.variant_rendering: the God of Amen (the God of truth)
verses.mt_rendering: the God of Amen (the God of truth)
variant_notes: The scroll uses plene spelling for elohei. The remarkable title 'God of Amen' (or 'God of truth/faithfulness') is identical in both texts. This is the only place in the Hebrew Bible where God is called by this title. The 'former troubles are forgotten and hidden from My eyes' prepares for the new creation vision that follows.

verses.mt_reading: כִּי הִנְנִי בוֹרֵא שָׁמַיִם חֲדָשִׁים וָאָרֶץ חֲדָשָׁה
verses.dss_reading: כיא הנני בורא שמים חדשים וארץ חדשה
verses.variant_rendering: For behold, I am creating new heavens and a new earth
verses.mt_rendering: For behold, I am creating new heavens and a new earth
variant_notes: The scroll spells ki as kia but the substantive reading is identical. This is one of the most consequential verses in the Hebrew Bible for eschatology. The phrase 'new heavens and a new earth' (shamayim chadashim va-arets chadashah) is echoed in 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1. The scroll — predating both NT texts by over a century — confirms this reading without variant.
The verb bore ('creating') uses the same root as Genesis 1:1 (bara), signaling that the new creation is an act of divine power equal to the original creation. Both texts preserve this connection.

verses.dss_reading: כיא הנני בורא את ירושלם גילה
verses.variant_rendering: for behold, I am creating Jerusalem a rejoicing
verses.mt_rendering: for behold, I am creating Jerusalem a rejoicing
variant_notes: The scroll spells ki as kia. The parallel creation of Jerusalem as 'a rejoicing and her people a joy' is identical. The new creation is not merely cosmic but specifically Zion-centered.

verses.variant_rendering: and I will rejoice in Jerusalem and be glad in My people
verses.mt_rendering: and I will rejoice in Jerusalem and be glad in My people
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. The declaration that weeping and crying shall no longer be heard is identical in both texts. This is directly echoed in Revelation 21:4: 'He will wipe away every tear from their eyes.'

verses.dss_reading: כיא הנער בן מאה שנה ימות
verses.variant_rendering: for the young man shall die at a hundred years
verses.mt_rendering: for the young man shall die at a hundred years
variant_notes: The scroll spells ki as kia and omits the maqqef. The vision of extended lifespans in the new creation — where dying at a hundred is considered dying young — is identical in both texts. The sinner who fails to reach a hundred is considered cursed. This suggests a transformed but not yet fully eschatological world, distinct from the 'no more death' of Revelation 21:4.
No significant variant. The promise of building houses and inhabiting them, planting vineyards and eating their fruit, is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: like the days of a tree shall be the days of My people
verses.mt_rendering: like the days of a tree shall be the days of My people
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. The comparison of human lifespan to tree lifespan is identical. This reverses the metaphor of 64:6 where humans 'fade like a leaf.'

verses.variant_rendering: they shall not labor in vain
verses.mt_rendering: they shall not labor in vain
variant_notes: The scroll spells lo with waw-aleph. The promise that labor will not be in vain and children will not be born for calamity is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: before they call I will answer
verses.mt_rendering: before they call I will answer
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. The intimate promise of divine responsiveness — 'before they call I will answer; while they are still speaking I will hear' — is identical in both texts.

verses.variant_rendering: the wolf and the lamb shall graze together
verses.mt_rendering: the wolf and the lamb shall graze together
variant_notes: No meaningful variant. This verse recapitulates the peaceable kingdom vision of Isaiah 11:6-9 but with notable differences: here the wolf and lamb 'graze together' (not 'dwell together'), and the lion eats straw 'like the ox.' The closing declaration — 'They shall not hurt or destroy in all My holy mountain, says the LORD' — directly quotes 11:9 and is preserved identically in both MT and the scroll.
The serpent eating dust ('dust shall be the serpent's food') echoes Genesis 3:14 and may signify the curse remaining on the serpent even in the new creation. Both texts agree.
